title: Loops & Nested Loops
description: Deep-dive into loop tracing, nested loop patterns, and the art of pattern printing — classic CSE110 exam territory. You will learn a systematic tracing technique for any loop, dissect how the inner and outer loop variables interact, and practice predicting the exact output of multi-level loop structures. Pattern printing is the ultimate test of whether you truly understand nested loops.

sections.content: Tracing a loop means **simulating the CPU**: evaluating every condition, executing every statement, and tracking every variable value. Do not try to 'shortcut' by guessing — follow each step mechanically. This technique works for any loop structure.

sections.label: The 3-Column Trace Table
sections.content: For any loop, draw a table with columns: **[iteration #]**, **[variable values before body]**, **[output/effect]**. Fill it row by row. This structured approach prevents the #1 mistake: losing track of variable state between iterations.

sections.title: Tracing a for Loop Step by Step

sections.content: In nested loops, the **inner loop completes entirely** before the outer loop advances by one step. Think of it like a clock: the inner loop is the seconds hand, the outer loop is the minutes hand.

sections.title: Nested Loop — Total Iteration Count

sections.label: Total Iterations Rule
sections.content: For a nested loop with outer range `m` and inner range `n`, the total iterations = `m × n`. For a **triangular** nested loop (`j <= i`), the total = `1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2`. These formulas let you estimate complexity at a glance.
🔺 Pattern Printing — The Ultimate Nested Loop Test

Pattern printing is a CSE110 staple because it forces you to think about the **relationship between `i` and `j`** and translate a visual shape into mathematical conditions. The key insight: the outer loop controls rows, the inner loop controls columns, and the **print condition** controls what goes in each cell.

sections.title: Pattern 1 — Right Triangle (Stars)
Pattern 2 — Inverted Triangle
Pattern 3 — Number Triangle
Pattern 4 — Square with Hollow Centre

sections.label: Pattern Printing Strategy
sections.content: Before coding any pattern: (1) Look at the pattern and identify what changes row by row. (2) Express the number of characters on row `i` as a formula in `i`. (3) If you print different characters, find the condition that distinguishes them (border? diagonal? even/odd column?). (4) Code outer loop for rows, inner loop for columns per row.

sections.title: Pattern 5 — Multiplication Table
Pattern 6 — Pyramid (Centered Triangle)

sections.content: These are the bugs that cause students to lose marks. Know each one by name and by its symptom.

rows: Loop runs N-1 or N+1 times
Carefully check: should the last value be included?
Ensure loop variable is modified toward termination
Variable not reset between outer iterations
Declare/reset inner variables inside the outer loop body
Trace one full iteration; check which variable controls which axis
Check if `println()` is inside or outside the outer loop
Never modify `i` inside the loop body unless intentional

sections.title: The 'Variable Not Reset' Bug — Classic Exam Trap
Exercise 1: Trace the Nested Loop Variables
sections.explanation: Key: inner loop starts at `j = i`, not `j = 1`. Row i=1: j goes 1,2,3 → print 11,12,13. Row i=2: j goes 2,3 → print 22,23. Row i=3: j goes 3 → print 33. This is an upper-triangular pattern. The inner loop shrinks each outer iteration because the start point advances.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Accumulator Inside Nested Loops
sections.answer:
i=1 rowSum=1
i=2 rowSum=3
i=3 rowSum=6
i=4 rowSum=10
total=20
sections.explanation: `rowSum` is reset to 0 each outer iteration. i=1: j=1, rowSum=1. i=2: j=1+2=3. i=3: j=1+2+3=6. i=4: j=1+2+3+4=10. These are triangular numbers: 1, 3, 6, 10. Total = 1+3+6+10 = 20.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Predict the Pattern Output
sections.explanation: Row i=1: j=1 (j<=1, print 1), j=2,3,4 (j>1, print '.') → '1 . . .'. Row i=2: j=1,2 print 2; j=3,4 print '.' → '2 2 . .'. Row i=3: j=1,2,3 print 3; j=4 '.' → '3 3 3 .'. Row i=4: all j<=4, print 4 four times → '4 4 4 4'. Lower-left triangle filled with the row number.

sections.title: Exercise 4: while Loop Tracing (Tricky Update)
sections.explanation: i starts at 1. Each iteration: print i, then i = i*2. Sequence: 1→2→4→8→16→32→64. Condition `i<=32`: 1✓ 2✓ 4✓ 8✓ 16✓ 32✓ 64✗(exit). So 1,2,4,8,16,32 are printed. After the loop, i=64 (the value that failed the condition). Final `println(i)` prints 64.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Print Diamond Pattern
sections.approach: Split into upper half (pyramid) and lower half (inverted pyramid). For n=5, upper has 3 rows (1 star, 3 stars... wait — n=5 means 3 rows of increasing stars: 1,3,5 if width = 2k-1; or if we count star groups: 1,2,3 stars for n=5). Let mid=(n+1)/2. Upper: i=1 to mid, spaces=(mid-i), stars=i. Lower: i=mid-1 down to 1, spaces=(mid-i), stars=i.
sections.explanation: For n=5, mid=3. Upper half (i=1 to 3): i=1 → 2 spaces + 1 star; i=2 → 1 space + 2 stars; i=3 → 0 spaces + 3 stars. Lower half (i=2 down to 1): i=2 → 1 space + 2 stars; i=1 → 2 spaces + 1 star. The diamond shape comes from the fact that the number of leading spaces = (mid - i) and it increases again in the lower half as i decreases.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Find the Off-by-One Error
sections.error: Logical Error (off-by-one): The condition `i < n` stops the loop at `i = n-1 = 4`. The number `n` itself (5) is never added. Output is `Sum 1 to 5: 10` (which is 1+2+3+4=10), not 15. The correct sum of 1..5 is 15.
sections.explanation: The loop `for (int i = 1; i < n; i++)` iterates with i = 1, 2, 3, 4 (stops when i=5 because 5 < 5 is false). To include n itself, use `i <= n`. This off-by-one (`<` vs `<=`) is the single most common loop bug in programming. **Rule of thumb**: If you want to include the last value, use `<=`. If the last value is a boundary to stop before, use `<`.

sections.label: Exam Pattern Approach
sections.content: In CSE110 exams, you may be asked to write OR trace pattern-printing code. For tracing: manually execute every iteration and write the output character by character. For writing: identify (1) number of rows, (2) characters per row as a function of row number, (3) spacing formula. Spending 2 minutes planning pays off in 5 minutes of correct code.
